title: Trauma and the Embodiment System: Returning Home to Your Body After Trauma
excerpt: Explore how trauma disrupts embodiment and learn science-based strategies to reconnect with your body for healing and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Trauma and the Embodiment System

Trauma doesn’t just affect our minds — it fundamentally alters how we inhabit and experience our bodies. The term embodiment system refers to the integrated process by which our brain, nervous system, and body interact to create our lived experience of being in physical form. When trauma occurs, this system can become fractured, leaving many feeling alienated from their own bodies.

To understand why reconnecting with your body is essential after trauma, we need to explore the science behind embodiment and trauma recovery. This article draws on the research and clinical insights of pioneers like Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, Stephen Porges, Deb Dana, Pat Ogden, Gabor Maté, Dan Siegel, Richard Schwartz, and Francine Shapiro to offer a clear, practical framework for returning home to your body.

What is Embodiment?

Embodiment is the process by which the brain and body work as a cohesive system to create self-awareness, emotional regulation, and a sense of agency. Neuroscientist Dan Siegel emphasizes that embodiment is the foundation of mindfulness and the integration of the mind and body, essential for mental health.

Pat Ogden, a somatic trauma therapist, defines embodiment as the “felt sense” of being alive in your body — an internal sensory experience that connects you to your emotions, physical sensations, and environment.

When embodiment is intact, you:

  • Feel grounded and present in your body
  • Can sense internal cues like hunger, tension, or calm
  • Have access to your emotional states without being overwhelmed
  • Experience a coherent sense of self

How Trauma Disrupts Embodiment

Trauma hijacks the nervous system and disrupts the natural flow of embodiment. According to Bessel van der Kolk, trauma is stored not just in the brain but in the body, often as chronic tension, numbness, or dysregulation. This is because trauma activates the survival circuits of the autonomic nervous system, primarily the sympathetic (fight/flight) and dorsal vagal (freeze/shutdown) branches.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma can cause the nervous system to become stuck in defensive states, shutting down the ability to engage safely with the world or even with your own bodily sensations. This leads to dissociation — a common trauma response where the body and mind disconnect to protect from overwhelming pain.

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, highlights that trauma traps energy in the body, preventing completion of natural defensive responses (like fight or flight). This leaves the body in a state of hyperarousal or hypoarousal, both of which impair embodiment.

Symptoms of Disembodiment After Trauma

When trauma disrupts embodiment, you might notice:

  • Feeling detached or numb from your body
  • Chronic pain or tension without medical explanation
  • Difficulty identifying or expressing emotions
  • Heightened startle response or hypervigilance
  • Memory gaps or flashbacks triggered by bodily sensations
  • Feeling like you’re observing yourself rather than living inside your body

Gabor Maté points out that these symptoms are not signs of weakness but natural protective adaptations. The body is trying to shield you from pain, but this protective mechanism comes at the cost of losing connection with yourself.

Why Reconnecting with Your Body Matters

Returning home to your body is a cornerstone of trauma recovery. Without embodiment, therapy can feel ineffective because the trauma remains trapped in the body. As Francine Shapiro, developer of EMDR therapy, notes, accessing the body’s sensations during trauma processing enhances healing by integrating the traumatic memories into the brain’s narrative circuits.

Deb Dana, a leading expert in Polyvagal Theory, stresses that restoring safety in the nervous system through body awareness is essential for trauma survivors to reclaim agency and resilience.

“The body keeps the score.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Science-Based Strategies to Re-Embody After Trauma

Here are evidence-backed approaches to help you reconnect with your body safely and effectively.

1. Somatic Experiencing

Developed by Peter Levine, Somatic Experiencing (SE) focuses on tracking bodily sensations to release trapped trauma energy. SE encourages gentle awareness of tension and urges in the body, allowing the nervous system to complete unfinished defensive responses.

2. Polyvagal-Informed Practices

Deb Dana’s work offers practical tools to engage the ventral vagal system — the branch of the nervous system that supports safety, social engagement, and calm. Techniques include paced breathing, vocalization, and safe social connection to downregulate threat responses.

3. Mindful Movement and Body Awareness

Practices such as yoga, tai chi, and qigong promote embodiment by cultivating nonjudgmental awareness of breath and movement. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy integrates movement and mindfulness to help survivors reclaim agency over their bodies.

4. Grounding Techniques

Simple grounding exercises, like feeling your feet on the floor or noticing textures, bring attention back to the present moment and the physical self. These are especially useful during flashbacks or dissociative episodes.

5. EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing)

Francine Shapiro’s EMDR has been shown to reduce trauma symptoms by integrating sensory and emotional information in the brain, often facilitated by bilateral stimulation that can include body-focused awareness.

6. Internal Family Systems (IFS)

Richard Schwartz’s IFS model helps survivors access different parts of themselves, including the wounded and protective inner voices, fostering internal harmony and improving embodiment by promoting self-compassion and acceptance.

Practical Tips for Self-Directed Embodiment Work

If you’ve tried talk therapy but still feel stuck, incorporating somatic tools can deepen your healing.

  • Start small: Spend 5 minutes daily noticing your breath or body sensations without judgment.
  • Track your triggers: Notice when you dissociate or feel numb and gently bring awareness to what’s happening physically.
  • Use safe movement: Gentle stretching or walking can help you reconnect without overwhelming your system.
  • Practice grounding: Engage your senses by holding objects, smelling essential oils, or listening to calming sounds.
  • Seek trauma-informed support: Working with a trained somatic therapist can guide you safely through this process.

The Road to Embodiment is a Journey

Reconnecting with your body after trauma is not a linear path. It requires patience, self-compassion, and sometimes professional guidance. The good news is that the body is inherently designed to heal and restore balance when given the right support.

By embracing the wisdom of your body and the science of trauma recovery, you reclaim your sense of self and agency. As Gabor Maté reminds us, healing comes from connection — especially the connection to your own living, breathing body.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). When the Body Says No: The Cost of Hidden Stress. Wiley.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the body differently than the mind?
faqs.a: Trauma impacts the nervous system and bodily sensations, often causing tension, numbness, or dysregulation that aren’t always accessible through conscious thought alone. The body stores trauma memories that can trigger automatic survival responses.

faqs.q: Why do some people feel disconnected from their bodies after trauma?
faqs.a: Dissociation serves as a protective mechanism where the brain and body disconnect to avoid overwhelming pain. While this helps survival, it disrupts embodiment and leaves one feeling alienated from physical sensations and emotions.

faqs.q: Can embodiment practices replace traditional therapy?
faqs.a: Embodiment practices complement but do not replace trauma-informed therapy. They enhance healing by addressing bodily stored trauma, but working with a trained professional ensures safety and effective integration.

faqs.q: What are simple ways to begin reconnecting with my body safely?
faqs.a: Start with gentle grounding exercises like feeling your feet on the floor, mindful breathing, or noticing textures around you. The key is to stay within your window of tolerance and avoid overwhelming sensations.

metaTitle: Trauma and Embodiment: Reconnect with Your Body

author: The Oracle Lover
